coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of BiOBr/carbon quantum dots microspheres with enhanced photoactivity and photostability under visible light irradiation
coredata.dc:creator: Zhao, Chenhui
Li, Wei
Liang, Ying
Tian, Yi
Zhang, Qiuyu
coredata.dc:description: In this paper, BiOBr/carbon quantum dots (CQDs) microspheres were successfully synthesized via a facile solvothermal method followed by a hydrothermal process. Then, the structures, morphologies, optical properties, and photocatalytic performances were investigated in detail. Research showed that the BiOBr/CQDs microspheres exhibited significantly enhanced photocatalytic performance as compared with pure BiOBr. When the CQDs introduction was appropriate, the highest photodegradation rate (0.0296min−1) would be obtained in the photocatalysis process of rhodamine B (RhB) which was about 5.3 times higher than that of pure BiOBr under visible light irradiation. As for p-Nitrotoluene (PNP), it could not be photodegraded by pure BiOBr while the BiOBr/CQDs microspheres displayed obvious photocatalytic activity. In this photocatalytic system, the enhanced photoactivity was mainly attributed to the fast interfacial charge transfer ability and enhanced light harvesting for the appropriate CQDs introduction.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Bismuth oxyhalides
Photocatalysis
Visible light
Organic pollutants

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst orcid primabst ref specialabst

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors are grateful for the financial support provided by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 51173146 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns of BiOBr/CQDs materials with different CQDs amounts.
XPS spectra of BiOBr and BiOBr/CQDs samples: (a) survey spectra, (b) Bi 4f, (c) O 1s, (d) Br 3d, and (e) C 1s core-level spectra.
FT-IR spectra of BiOBr/CQDs samples with different CQDs contents.
TG curves of BiOBr/CQDs materials with different CQDs amounts in nitrogen atmosphere.
SEM and TEM images of (a–d) BiOBr and (e–h) BiOBr/CQDs-0.6.
Nitrogen absorption-desorption isotherms of BiOBr and BiOBr/CQDs-0.6 materials.
(a) UV–vis spectra of BiOBr/CQDs materials with different contents of CQDs. (b) (αhv)1/2 vs hv curves.
PL spectra of BiOBr/CQDs hybrid materials with different CQDs amounts.

(a and c) Photodegradation kinetic curves and (b and d) fitted Langmuir-Hinshelwood models for the degradation of RhB and PNP with pure BiOBr and BiOBr/CQDs materials under visible light irradiation. (e) Cycling runs for the photodegradation of RhB in the presence of BiOBr/CQDs-0.6. materials under visible light irradiation.
UV–vis absorption spectra of RhB solution in trapping experiments by BiOBr/CQDs materials at 145min illumination.
Schematic of the separation and transfer of photogenerated carriers in the BiOBr/CQDs material combined with the possible reaction mechanism of photocatalytic procedure.

head.ce:title: Synthesis of BiOBr/carbon quantum dots microspheres with enhanced photoactivity and photostability under visible light irradiation

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Applied Chemistry, College of Science, Northwestern Polytechnical University, Xi’an 710072, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Space Physics and Chemistry, Ministry of Education, Northwestern Polytechnical University, P. O. Box 624, Xi’an 710072, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Space Physics and Chemistry

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: Department of Applied Chemistry, College of Science, Northwestern Polytechnical University, Xi’an 710072, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this paper, BiOBr/carbon quantum dots (CQDs) microspheres were successfully synthesized via a facile solvothermal method followed by a hydrothermal process. Then, the structures, morphologies, optical properties, and photocatalytic performances were investigated in detail. Research showed that the BiOBr/CQDs microspheres exhibited significantly enhanced photocatalytic performance as compared with pure BiOBr. When the CQDs introduction was appropriate, the highest photodegradation rate (0.0296min−1) would be obtained in the photocatalysis process of rhodamine B (RhB) which was about 5.3 times higher than that of pure BiOBr under visible light irradiation. As for p-Nitrotoluene (PNP), it could not be photodegraded by pure BiOBr while the BiOBr/CQDs microspheres displayed obvious photocatalytic activity. In this photocatalytic system, the enhanced photoactivity was mainly attributed to the fast interfacial charge transfer ability and enhanced light harvesting for the appropriate CQDs introduction.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, photocatalysis technology, offers a “green” technology for completely eliminating various kinds of contaminants (especially some azodyes) and obtaining ideal hydrogen energy from sunlight and water [1–4], is considered as a very important method for relieving or resolving current environmental issue and energy crisis. Particularly, bismuth oxyhalides (BiOX, X=Cl, Br, and I) have been widely studied due to their excellent photoelectric property and catalytic performance [5–11]. Among numerous BiOX semiconductors, BiOBr was greatly investigated for its appropriate band gap and excellent visible light photocatalytic activity [12–14]. However, the photocatalytic acticity of the pure BiOBr semiconductor was limited by its low separation efficiency of the photoexcited electron-hole pairs. In order to improve photocatalytic activity of BiOBr semiconductor, BiOBr has been modified by SiO2 [15], Fe3O4/mSiO2 [16], MoS2 [17], BiOI [18–21], Bi24O31Br10 [22], NiFe2O4/BiOBr [23], WO3/Bi2WO6 [24], Bi2O3 [25], nitrogen-doped graphene [26], BiPO4 [27], TiO2 [28,29], Bi2WO6 [30,31], Ag3PO4 [32], CoFe2O4 [33], CdS [34] and Ag2CO3 [35] etc. However, these large-size materials can hardly build perfect interacted interfaces with BiOBr, the surface defects could act as recombination centers of photoexcited electron-hole pairs which may limit the photocatalytic activity [36,37].

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), a new class of carbon nanomaterials with size less than 10nm, were first obtained during purification of single-walled carbon nanotubes through preparative electrophoresis in 2004 [38]. As a group of newly emerged fluorescent nanomaterials, CQDs have shown tremendous potential as versatile nanomaterials for a wide range of applications, including chemical sensing, biosensing, bioimaging, drug delivery, photodynamic therapy, photocatalysis and electrocatalysis [39]. Compared to conventional semiconductor quantum dots, the unique attributes of CQDs, for example their benign chemical composition, tunable fluorescence emissions, facile functionalization and excellent physicochemical and photochemical stability (non-photobleaching or non-photoblinking), render them very attractive for technical applications [39]. Together with other advantages such as low cost and ease of synthesis [40], CQDs are in a favorable position for achieving unprecedented performance.

CQDs are typically quasi-spherical nanoparticles comprising amorphous to nanocrystalline cores with predominantly graphitic or turbostratic carbon (sp2 carbon) or graphene and graphene oxide sheets fused by diamond-like sp3 hybridised carbon insertions [39], which provide the CQDs with superior electron transfer and reservoir ability [37]. In addition, the up-converted photoluminescence (PL) behavior of CQDs can expand the light utilization range of nanomaterials from UV to visible range and enhance photo-induced electron transfer [39]. Therefore, a series of CQDs-based photocatalysts have been synthesized to improve the photocatalytic activity of semiconductors, such as CQDs/Cu2O [41], CQDs/Fe2O3 [42], CQDs/Bi2MoO6 [43], CQDs/TiO2 [44], CQDs/Ag3PO4 [45], CQDs/Fe3O4 [46] and CQDs/Bi2WO6 [36]. However, the binding form between CQDs and semiconductors as well as functional mechanism of CQDs for photocatalytic activity improvement is far from clear. Therefore, it was highly desirable to solve the problems aforementioned in order to further improve the performance of CQDs by appropriate means so as to acquire satisfactory high-efficient photocatalytic activity of CQDs-based materials.

Considering the superiorities of BiOBr and CQDs above mentioned, the BiOBr/CQDs microspheres were successfully prepared via a facile hydrothermal route followed by a hydrothermal process. The structures, morphologies, optical properties were investigated in detail, and the photocatalytic activities of BiOBr/CQDs materials were also evaluated sufficiently by the photocatalytic degradation of two different kinds of model pollutants (RhB and PNP). The relationships between the structure of the photocatalyst and the photocatalytic activities were also discussed in detail. Ultimately, the mechanism of pollutant photodegradation in this system was studied and proposed.
All chemicals were purchased from J&K Chemical and were used as received.

The BiOBr microspheres were synthesized by a facile solvothermal method. In a typical procedure, 14mmol Bi (NO3)3·5H2O and 10mmol KBr was dissolved in 80mL of ethylene glycol by ultrasonic. Then, the solution was transferred into a 100mL teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and kept at 180°C for 8h. After the reaction finished, the resulting samples were collected and separated by centrifugation and washed with deionized water and absolute ethanol followed by drying via the vacuum freeze-drying technology [47,48].

The synthesis of BiOBr/CQDs materials was carried out according to a previous report with little modification [49]. Firstly, 1.0g as-prepared BiOBr microspheres were dispersed in 100mL deionized water by ultrasonic for about 5min. Then the stoichiometric (0.2g, 0.4g, 0.6g, 0.8g and 1.0g) citric acid and 2.68mL ethylenediamine was added into the above system under continuous mechanical stirring. The above mixture was transferred into 100mL teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and heated at 180°C for 8h and cooled down to room temperature. The acquired product was subjected to centrifugation and washed several times with deionized water and absolute ethanol followed by drying via the vacuum freeze-drying technology centrifugation. The resulting sample was coded as BiOBr/CQDs-α, and α denotes the mass of citric acid.

The crystalline phases of BiOBr/CQDs materials were determined by X-ray power diffraction (XRD) analysis using a Bruker AXS D8-advance X-ray diffractometer with Cu-Kα radiation. The elements and chemical states on the surface of BiOBr/CQDs materials were determined by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) using an Axis Ultra instrument (Kratos Analytical, Manchester, U.K.) under ultrahigh vacuum condition (<10−6 Pa) with a monochromatic Al Kα X-ray source (1486.6eV). Fourier transform-infrared (FT-IR) spectra were measured on a PerkinElmer 580BIR spectrophotometer using KBr pellet technique. Thermogravimetric analysis (TG) was performed on a Q5000IR (TA Instruments, US) apparatus. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) micrographs were observed on a JEOL-JSM-6700F. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) micrographs were observed on a JEOL JEM-2100F. N2 adsorption/desorption isotherms were obtained on a TriStar II 20 apparatus. UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS) were performed on a Shimadzu UV-3600 spectrometer (BaSO4 power as substrate). The Photoluminescence (PL) spectra of BiOBr/CQDs materials were detected on a Varian Cary Eclipse spectrometer.

The photocatalytic activity of the as-prepared BiOBr/CQDs materials with different CQDs contents was investigated by the photodegradation of 20mg/L RhB and 20mg/L PNP under visible light (a 300W Xe arc lamp with a UV cutoff filter (400nm)). In a typical process, 100mg BiOBr/CQDs samples were added into 100mL RhB or 100mL PNP. Prior to irradiation, the suspension was stirred in the dark for 30min to ensure adsorption-desorption equilibrium. During the photodegradation process, 3mL suspension was sampled at certain time intervals and centrifuged at 13000rpm for 3min to remove the BiOBr/CQDs microspheres for analysis. The concentration of RhB and PNP samples were analyzed on an UV–vis spectrophotometer (UV-2450, Shimadzu) at wavelength of 553nm and 317nm, respectively.

The phases of the BiOBr/CQDs microspheres with different CQDs contents were investigated by X-ray diffraction (XRD), and the diffraction spectra were shown in Fig. 1 . The peaks located at 10.9°, 25.3°, 31.8°, 32.3°, 33.3°, 39.4°, 40.6°, 47.0° and 56.4° can be indexed to the (001), (011), (012), (110), (003), (112), (013), (113) and (114) crystal planes of tetragonal BiOBr, respectively (JCPDS card No. 73-2061) [37]. After the introduction of CQDs, some new peaks appeared at 23.9°, 27.2°, 38.0° and 39.6° which could be indexed to the (101), (012), (104) and (110) crystal planes of rhombohedral Bi (JCPDS Card No.44-1246) [52]. It should be transformed from the BiOBr matrix during the growth process of CQDs under high temperature and pressure, and it may exhibit superior surface plasma resonance (SPR) effect in photocatalytic process. Furthermore, the diffraction peaks of the (110), (020) and (014) crystal planes of BiOBr were red-shifted from 32.17°, 46.33° and 50.70° to 32.63°, 46.91° and 51.97°, respectively. It indicated that the interacted interfaces were successfully formed between BiOBr and CQDs [53]. No typical diffraction peaks of CQDs were detected, which could be explained by its low amount in BiOBr/CQDs materials. Similar results had been found in other systems [45,50].

Furthermore, XPS spectra of BiOBr and BiOBr/CQDs microspheres were used to confirm the element composition and valence states. The survey spectra (in Fig. 2 a) indicates that BiOBr contains Bi, O, Br and C elements, while the BiOBr/CQDs materials contains all aforementioned elements except Br. It can be attributed to the reduction of the amount of Br element during the conversion process from BiOBr to elemental Bi which would weaken the signal of Br. In addition, the cover of CQDs on BiOBr surface may hinder the detecting of Br. As for the high-resolution spectra of Bi (Fig. 2b), two peaks located at 159.1 and 164.4eV can be assigned to the Bi 4f7/2 and Bi 4f5/2 of Bi3+ in BiOBr, respectively. In BiOBr/CQDs material, the Bi 4f peaks shift to 156.7 and 162.0eV, respectively. Furthermore, the peaks located at 157.5 and 162.7eV can be attributed to Bi 4f7/2 and Bi 4f5/2 of elemental Bi [54], respectively, which is consistent with the results of XRD. The O 1s spectrum in BiOBr (Fig. 2c) can be fitted into three peaks located at 530.0, 531.2 and 532.1eV (which are attributed to O in the BiOBr and other components, such as OH and H2O)[55], while the O 1s peaks in BiOBr/CQDs material shift to 527.7, 529.7 and 531.2eV, respectively. The Br 3d spectrum in BiOBr (Fig. 2d) can be fitted into two peaks located at 68.2 and 69.2eV, while the Br 3d peaks in BiOBr/CQDs material shift to 65.5 and 66.6eV, respectively, indicating the existence of the interaction between the interfaces of CQDs and BiOBr. Fig. 2e displays the high resolution XPS spectra of the C 1s in samples. The peaks at 281.8, 282.9, 285.6eV can be assigned to adventitious carbon species from XPS measurements [56], while the peaks located at 282.6, 283.9 and 286.4eV can be attributed to CC/CC, oxygenated carbon and nitrous carbon for C 1s in BiOBr/CQDs material, respectively [49]. The XPS analysis indicates that the intimate integration of CQDs and BiOBr has been constructed in BiOBr/CQDs materials.

Fig. 3 shows the FI-IR spectra of BiOBr and BiOBr/CQDs materials with different CQDs loading amounts. The peak at 510cm−1 is associated with the BiO stretching mode in the BiOBr materials [37], whereas it shifts to 548cm−1 with respect to BiOBr/CQDs samples, indicating the existence of the interaction between CQDs and BiOBr [36,57]. For the BiOBr/CQDs materials, the absorption peak assigned to the absorption peaks of COO− appears at 1388cm−1 [51,58], revealing the existence of CQDs [36,51,58]. In addition, the absorption peaks located at 3432cm−1 and 2922cm−1 can be assigned to the stretching vibrations of OH and CH [36]. The FT-IR result shows that CQDs and BiOBr had been successfully coupled together which is consistent with the XPS analysis.

Besides, thermal gravity analysis (TG) was carried out to further determine the presence of CQDs in BiOBr/CQDs materials. As shown in Fig. 4 , the trace weight loss before 100°C can be attributed to the physically absorbed water in the samples [57]. During the growth process of the CQDs, the porous structure was formed in the BiOBr matrix, which had been demonstrated by the N2 adsorption-desorption characterization (Fig. S4 and Fig. S5). With the increase of the CQDs introduction, the BET surface area would increase accordingly (Table S1), thus more water vapor would be adsorbed on the surface of the sample. The weight loss at the range of 100–200°C could be attributed to loss of some small molecules. The weight loss of the samples from 200°C to 500°C might be ascribed to the introduced CQDs. In addition, the total weight loss ratio is about 0.4%, 2.6%, 4.8%, 6%, 7.3% and 10.9% as α (BiOBr/CQDs-α) increases from 0 to 1.0, that is to say the weight loss increases with the increase of the introduced CQDs amounts, which demonstrates the CQDs have been introduced successfully. TG curves of BiOBr/CQDs materials in air atmosphere (Fig. S1) show that weight loss curves is similar with those in nitrogen atmosphere except that the weight loss ratio is higher, which may be attributed to total removal of CQDs by sufficient burning.

The morphology and microstructure of the BiOBr and BiOBr/CQDs materials were characterized by SEM and TEM. Fig. 5 a and b shows the SEM images of BiOBr. The sample exhibits the representative microsphere-like structure assembled by numerous BiOBr nanosheets, and the microspheres average size is about 3μm (Fig. 5a and b).The TEM images of pure BiOBr (Fig. 5c) shows that some nanosheets can be found from the edge of sphere-like structure, which also demonstrates that BiOBr microsphere is composed of nanosheets. High-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image of BiOBr (Fig. 5d) shows that the lattice fringe spacing of 0.280nm and 0.350nm corresponding to the (012) crystal plane and (011) crystal plane of tetragonal BiOBr (JCPDS 73-2061) can be observed, respectively [59]. Fig. 5e and f display the SEM images of BiOBr/CQDs materials. Compared with BiOBr, a similar microsphere-like morphology with the average diameter of about 3μm can be observed, which is also consists of ultrathin BiOBr nanosheets (Fig. 5f). These results imply that the introduction of CQDs does not change the main morphology of BiOBr. The TEM analysis is applied to further explore the microstructures of BiOBr/CQDs materials (Fig. 5g, h). Similarly, the TEM image of BiOBr/CQDs materials shows that some nanosheets can be found from the edge of the sphere-like structure. At the same time, the HRTEM image of the BiOBr/CQDs material (Fig. 5h) shows that there are a lot of dark dots with the diameter of about 6nm evenly attached to the surface of BiOBr nanosheets, which implies that the CQDs have been successfully anchored on the BiOBr nanosheets and intimate integration has been constructed. In addition, the density of CQDs would increase with the increase of CQDs content (Fig. S2). Fig. 5h also shows that the lattice spacing of 0.320nm and 0.328nm can be observed, which can be attributed to the (002) crystal plane of CQDs [36,45] and (012) crystal plane of Bi [52]. Some CQDs do not show the obvious lattice fringe spacing due to the CQDs are quasi-spherical nanoparticles comprising amorphous to nanocrystalline core [36,49]. At the same time, the lattice spacing around 0.280nm can be observed, which agrees well with the crystallographic (012) spacing of BiOBr. The above results further demonstrate that the CQDs are successfully loaded on the BiOBr to construct BiOBr/CQDs materials. Furthermore, the energy dispersion X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) analysis (Fig. S3) shows that the BiOBr/CQDs samples contain C, O, Br and Bi element, which reveals that the BiOBr/CQDs materials have been successfully synthesized.

The N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms (Fig. 6 ) of the BiOBr and BiOBr/CQDs-0.6 materials were used to further study their microstructures. The BET surface area of the BiOBr/CQDs-0.6 (23.6525m2/g) is larger than pure BiOBr (6.6575m2/g). In addition, Fig. S4 and Table S1 shows that BET surface areas of the BiOBr/CQDs increase with the increase of CQDs amounts which can be attributed to the formation of the porous structure during the CQDs introduced process (Fig. S5). In addition, the increased BET surface area of BiOBr/CQDs also benefits from the excellent adsorptive performance of CQDs [37]. It has been widely recognized that a higher BET surface area is advantageous for absorb more active species and reactants on its surface, which thus favors the improvement of photocatalytic performance [15,36,50].

Photoabsorption capability is an important factor influencing the photocatalytic performance of photocatalysts [15,55,60–62], and is usually evaluated by UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS). As shown in Fig. 7 a, the absorption edges of BiOBr/CQDs materials show red shifts compared with BiOBr, which means that the optical absorption in visible light is enhanced. The DRS results suggest that CQDs may play an important role in utilizing visible light and such produce more photoexcited e− -h+ pairs [17,51]. The band gap of BiOBr/CQDs materials were estimated via the classical Tauc approach [63–65]. As shown in Fig. 7b, the band gap of BiOBr/CQDs materials become smaller than that of BiOBr. A narrower band gap means a higher photocatalytic performance in visible light [36].

Photoluminescence (PL) intensity and lifetime of photocatalysts are capable of characterizing indirectly the recombination efficiency of photogenerated e− and h+
[55,66,67]. As is known, weaker intensity represents lower efficiency of e− and h+ recombination [63,68]. As shown in Fig. 8 , all the materials have strong emission peak centered between 370 and 400nm with an excitation wavelength of 380nm. BiOBr/CQDs materials display lower PL intensity than that of pure BiOBr, indicating the decreasing recombination efficiency of e− and h+ . This phenomenon can be ascribed to the highly efficient photoexcited carrier transfer ability between the interacted interface of CQDs and BiOBr, leading to the effective separation of the e− and h+
[37].

The photocatalytic activity of the as-prepared BiOBr/CQDs materials was first evaluated for the degradation of RhB under visible light irradiation (Fig. 9 a). The absorption-desorption balances were achieved between the catalyst and dye in 30min before photocatalysis. It can be seen that the direct photolysis of RhB is negligible without adding photocatalyst. In the presence of photocatalyst, the BiOBr/CQDs materials show much higher photocatalytic activity than that of pure BiOBr. The BiOBr/CQDs-0.6 material displays the highest photocatalytic activity. Nearly all of the RhB was photodegraded by BiOBr/CQDs-0.6 after 145min irradiation, while only 57% of RhB was removed by pure BiOBr. Then, the corresponding fitted Langmuir-Hinshelwood models were obtained (Fig. 9b), and it is easy to see that the photodegradation of RhB obeys pseudo-first-order kinetics well. It can be seen that the BiOBr/CQDs-0.6 materials has the maximum rate constant of 0.0296min−1, which is about 5.3 times higher than pure BiOBr (k=0.0056min−1). When α is higher than 0.6, BiOBr/CQDs materials show a decreased photocatalytic activity for the RhB degradation. The results indicate that the proper introduction of the CQDs is beneficial for the improvement of the photocatalytic performance of BiOBr/CQDs materials. Too many CQDs on the surface of BiOBr would limit the light absorption of BiOBr, leading to lower photocatalytic activity. Similar results can also been found in the literatures [36,50,51].

PNP, a common refractory organic pollutant in waste water, was chosen to further evaluate the photocatalytic activity of BiOBr/CQDs materials under visible light irradiation, and the results were shown in Fig. 9c. Similarly, the absorption-desorption balances were achieved in 30min before photocatalysis. It can be seen that PNP has been hardly degraded over pure BiOBr for 520min, which indicates that BiOBr microspheres are not an efficient photocatalyst for PNP degradation. However, BiOBr/CQDs materials exhibited a significant photocatalytic activity for the degradation of PNP with BiOBr/CQDs-0.6 showing the highest photocatalytic activity (k=0.0007min−1), and 26% of PNP was decomposed by the BiOBr/CQDs-0.6 during 320min. The photocatalytic degradation kinetics of PNP also follow the pseudo-first-order kinetics plot (Fig. 9d). These experimental data also reveals that the proper introduction of CQDs are beneficial for the improvement of the photocatalytic performance of BiOBr, and the BiOBr/CQDs materials are efficient visible-light-driven photocatalyst for the PNP degradation, making them valuable photocatalytic materials in environmental remediation.

Recycling reactions were carried out for the photodegradation of RhB to evaluate the photostability of BiOBr/CQDs-0.6 materials (collected after every 145min photocatalytic reaction) under visible light irradiation. As shown in Fig. 9e, the photocatalytic activity could still be maintained after four cycles with only 2.2% decrease of the degradation efficiency, which reveals that the BiOBr/CQDs material has high photostability during the photocatalysis process. Meanwhile, XRD spectra (Fig. S6) and TEM images (Fig. S7) indicate that the regenerated photocatalyst exhibits excellent physical stability.

In an attempt to elucidate the photocatalytic mechanism involved in the photodegradation of RhB by BiOBr/CQDs materials, a series of photodegradation experiments with the addition of different scavengers were carried out. The absorption peak value of RhB at 553nm analyzed by UV–vis spectrophotometer could indirectly reflect the degradation efficiency after being illuminated for 145min (Fig. 10 ). It can be seen that the photodegradation was completed at 145min illumination when TBA (e− acceptor) was added into the degradation system, demonstrating that e− does not have direct effect to the photocatalytic process. Similarly, when IPA ( OH scavenger) was added into the degradation system, the photodegradation efficiency of 90% was obtained during 145min illumination, which indicates that OH radical is not the main active species. However, photodegradation activity decreased by 17% during 145min illumination when KI (h+ scavenger) was added into the degradation system. Simultaneously, photodegradation activity decreased by 81% when BQ (O2
− scavenger) was added into the degradation system. This indicates that h+ and O2
− play an important role in the photodegradation process.

Based on the experimental results in this system, the photocatalytic mechanism was proposed in Fig. 11 . Firstly, due to the superior visible light response of CQDs, the photoexcited e− is generated when the sample is illuminated by visible light [69]. Then, the CQDs acting as electron donors, the photoexcited e− can be injected into the conduction band (CB) of BiOBr, and the photogenerated h+ can be injected into the valence band (VB) of CQDs from the VB of BiOBr. In this process, the photoexcited e− can be captured by O2 molecules in the system to generate the O2
− radicals [36,51]. Secondly, the up-converted PL property of CQDs can convert longer wavelength light to shorter wavelength light, which can in turn excite BiOBr to form photoexcited e− -h+ pairs [39]. Thirdly, for the formation of the interacted interfaces between BiOBr and CQDs, the delocalized conjugated structure of CQDs made it easier to transfer the photogenerated carriers, resulting in efficient e− -h+ pair separation [70]. In addition, the elemental Bi particles, generated in the BiOBr matrix during the growth process of CQDs, exhibit superior SPR effect and can further promote the formation of e− -h+ pairs in this photocatalyst system. Meanwhile, elemental Bi could act as electron donors, the photoexcited e− can be injected into the CB of BiOBr [52]. For the RhB degradation, the RhB could absorb the visible light to produce singlet and triplet states (denoted as RhB*) and the e− could also inject into the photocatalyst system from the photosensitive RhB [51,71]. In this process, the intermediate product RhB* can be oxidized by the O2
− radicals and photogenerated h+ to transform into the ultimate degradation products.

In summary, a novel BiOBr/CQDs hybrid material was successfully prepared through a facile solvothermal method followed by a hydrothermal process. The CQDs were dispersed on the surface of microsphere-like BiOBr and tight junctions were formed. The photocatalytic activities of BiOBr/CQDs materials on RhB and PNP degradation under visible light irradiation increased dramatically. The BiOBr/CQDs-0.6 exhibited the optimal photocatalytic performance for the degradation of RhB and PNP. Furthermore, the BiOBr/CQDs materials showed excellent reusability and stability, which was valuable for the potential applications of environmental protection. The enhanced photocatalytic performance of BiOBr/CQDs material was attributed to excellent electron transfer ability and the increased light harvesting capacity of CQDs. The radicals trapping experiments revealed that O2
− and the holes were the main active species during the photocatalysis process. This work was expectedly to provide appropriate theoretical reference for the new development of CQDs-based hybrid materials with high photocatalytic performance.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are grateful for the financial support provided by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 51173146).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.apcata.2016.09.005.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:
